paragraphs.text_preview: Purpose

To propose an updated version of the Digital Audit Benchmark Platform project following the initial Financial Audit Executive discussion on 16 April 2026, incorporating FAE feedback to refine scope, governance and delivery approach, and to progress the develop
What we have proposed and why it matters

In the previous version, we proposed an Audit Officeled pilot to simulate an AIpowered, datadriven financial audit workflow, using NSW local government audits as the initial test case. The proposal focused on experimenting across the full financial audit cycle

This matters because financial audits, particularly in the local government sector, remain labourintensive and constrained by manual processes and poor data access, creating risks to timeliness and efficiency. Without a structured way to test emerging technolo
Key Feedback from the FAE

The Data61 paper was seen as a useful exploratory discussion paper illustrating what is technically possible, but not as a decision paper or a signal of imminent implementation or organisational change.
FAE supported positioning CSIRO Data61 as strategic advisors with strong market and research insight, rather than committing early to a heavy internal build or a single technical solution.

Ethical considerations were acknowledged as necessary constraints under NSW Government settings, but should be reframed so they inform go / no‑go decisions on AI use without dominating the narrative or being interpreted as workforce replacement.
FAE advised clearly separating the technical simulation work from broader organisational and workforce considerations, noting that organisational redesign is not an expected output of this project.

There was strong concern about workforce messaging risk, with clear feedback that the narrative must focus on productivity enabled by efficiency, upskilling and reskilling, and not workforce reduction.

The paper should be explicitly anchored to existing Office and Auditor‑General strategies, including workforce, digital audit and IT strategies, noting that the strategic direction is already endorsed in principle.

Overall, FAE expressed near‑unanimous support for the direction, with remaining focus on practical matters such as cost, outputs, governance and design, and agreed the proposal should be refined and brought back.
What is in scope
Establishing a financial audit workflow to inform identification of opportunities where data and AI solutions will be relevant, specifically:
simulation of selected financial audit activities using historical or sandboxed data
local government audits as the initial test case
AI‑assisted capabilities such as:
transaction analytics and anomaly detection
document review and evidence classification
AI‑supported sampling and compliance checks
Human–AI collaboration models where:
auditors retain all judgement, sign‑off and accountability
AI outputs are auditable, explainable and reviewable

Development of an internally led minimum viable platform concept, built within the Audit Office environment, with CSIRO Data61 providing technical and advisory support to inform learning and benchmarking.
What is out of scope
Live deployment on active financial audits
Replacement of auditors or audit sign‑off
Mandating AI use across audits
Procurement of a commercial audit platform at this stage
Public release of tools or findings without executive approval
Strategic Alignment
Governance and project team (proposed)
Office Executive sponsor group
Brad Medina (Assistant Auditor-General – Local Government, Financial Audit Branch)
Scott Stanton (Assistant Auditor-General, Systems Assurance, Cyber and Data Branch)
Xiaoyan Lu (Executive Director, Corporate, Experience and Strategy Branch)
Internal team
Nathan Carter (Project lead)
Barry Li (Deputy project lead)
Internal contributors
Data and AI team
Digital Audit Working Group (FAB)
IT (platform, security, architecture)
External partners and advisors
CSIRO Data61 (research and benchmarking partner)
Other audit firms or vendors (knowledge sharing, tooling insights as appropriate)

Audit Office Corporate Plan – Strategic Objectives 2025–2028

The initiative supports the Corporate Plan objectives to transform public sector auditing, build futureready capability, and amplify public value by strengthening audit quality, insight and productivity through responsib

The proposal aligns with the Strategy’s focus on humanintheloop AI, safe experimentation, centralised data platforms and progressive digital uplift, while deliberately positioning the work as exploratory rather than oper

The pilot simulation is consistent with the intended direction of the Technology Strategy by using nonproduction environments to test and inform futurestate technology decisions without committing to premature solutions.

The initiative supports the emerging Workforce Strategy by providing a controlled environment for auditors to build digital, data and analytical capability, reinforcing upskilling and professional growth rather than work
